hyperedges.label: Secret lifecycle — register, use, rotate, rollback, audit
Enclave call pipeline — seal → grant egress → contract forward → http::call
T3 API probing scripts — discovery of correct T3 control-plane shapes
Secret Sealing Pipeline (plaintext enters once, sealed into enclave, metadata logged)
Secret Consumption Paths (proxy HTTP path + programmatic release path)
Observability & Audit Surface (dashboard reads both logs, reconciles against enclave)
prompt injection exfiltration chain
hyperedges.detail: End-to-end attack: injection_page hides payload → mock_openai_server obeys it → demo_tools get_env reads the key + http_get sends it → attacker_server receives the exfiltrated value. Blindfold breaks this chain at get_env (returns sentinel instead of real key).

hyperedges.label: cross-SDK sentinel proof
hyperedges.detail: Four examples across three SDKs (OpenAI Node, OpenAI Python, LangChain, Anthropic) and two languages proving the same one-line sentinel pattern works universally: set apiKey=__BLINDFOLD__ + baseURL=proxy.

hyperedges.label: demo infrastructure components
hyperedges.detail: Four local HTTP servers that together simulate a complete attack scenario without real LLM calls or real attacker infrastructure. All bind to 127.0.0.1:0 (random port), are started/stopped per demo run.

hyperedges.label: Blindfold Security Property Proven End-to-End
hyperedges.description: The core security property: sealed secret read inside TDX, sentinel substituted, outbound call made from enclave, agent process holds zero secrets — verified live against T3 testnet with real API calls

hyperedges.label: Root Cause Investigation Chain
hyperedges.description: Two independent root causes resolved: (1) API key with no provisioned tenant caused all 500s misdiagnosed as outage; (2) stub http.response headers field mismatch broke contract instantiation

hyperedges.label: Four Usage Modes (increasing trust level)
hyperedges.description: Spectrum from no-code CLI (secret in child process env) through proxy and release-broker (secret briefly in broker) to in-enclave http::call (secret never leaves enclave)

nodes.label: Blindfolded OpenAI call (Python) — with a proof this process never holds the key
nodes.norm_label: blindfolded openai call (python) — with a proof this process never holds the key

nodes.label: TOOL_ENV — known CLI tool → env var mapping
nodes.norm_label: tool_env — known cli tool → env var mapping

nodes.label: Sentinel substitution pattern — __BLINDFOLD__ replaced with real secret in-enclave
nodes.rationale: The sentinel value __BLINDFOLD__ acts as a placeholder in headers. It is substituted with the real secret only inside the TDX enclave, ensuring the plaintext never exists in the caller's process. This is the core security invariant of the entire Blindfold system.
nodes.norm_label: sentinel substitution pattern — __blindfold__ replaced with real secret in-enclave

nodes.label: Enclave-egress pattern — outbound HTTPS from inside TDX
nodes.rationale: The contract makes the real outbound HTTP call from within the TDX enclave. The plaintext secret never leaves the enclave process — it exists only on the stack between read_secret() and http::call(). This eliminates the attack surface where a compromised agent could intercept the secret in transit.
nodes.norm_label: enclave-egress pattern — outbound https from inside tdx

nodes.label: Release-broker pattern — plaintext returned to authenticated tenant only
nodes.rationale: When in-enclave HTTP is not available or practical (e.g. SMTP), release_to_tenant returns the plaintext to the authenticated tenant over the T3-encrypted session. The local broker uses it once and drops it. This is the fallback path when enclave-egress cannot be used.
nodes.norm_label: release-broker pattern — plaintext returned to authenticated tenant only

nodes.label: T3 SDK authentication pattern — handshake + authenticate + TenantClient
nodes.rationale: Every script that interacts with T3 follows the same pattern: import SDK, setEnvironment, eth_get_address, create T3nClient, handshake(), authenticate(), then create TenantClient. This is repeated across 12+ scripts, forming a shared boilerplate pattern.
nodes.norm_label: t3 sdk authentication pattern — handshake + authenticate + tenantclient

nodes.label: Egress grant via tee:user/contracts agent-auth-update
nodes.rationale: T3 denies outbound HTTP by default (host/http.egress_denied). To allow it, the tenant must call t3n.execute with script_name tee:user/contracts and function agent-auth-update, listing allowedHosts. This was discovered through trial-and-error probing (grant-egress.ts tried 13 shapes before finding the right one).

nodes.label: T3ClientHandle Interface (seedSecret, invokeForward, releaseSecret, verifySecret, grantEgress)
nodes.norm_label: t3clienthandle interface (seedsecret, invokeforward, releasesecret, verifysecret, grantegress)

nodes.label: Mock T3 Client (local stub for dev/CI)
nodes.norm_label: mock t3 client (local stub for dev/ci)

nodes.label: run-demo.ts
nodes.summary: Side-by-side Blindfold demo runner: executes Agent A (unprotected) and Agent B (blindfolded) against the identical prompt-injection attack, prints a verdict table comparing outcomes.

nodes.label: demo shared types
nodes.summary: Shared demo type definitions: Message, ToolCall, ModelOutput, ModelClient interface (step method), ToolBox, AgentConfig (label/model/tools/task/systemPrompt/maxTurns), and AgentResult (finalAnswer/turnsTaken/toolCalls).

nodes.label: demo toolbox (makeTools)
nodes.summary: Agent toolbox factory (makeTools): provides get_env (reads process.env), http_get (fetches URLs via fetch), and print tools. The get_env+http_get combination is the deliberate attack surface that enables key exfiltration.

nodes.label: injection-page.ts
nodes.summary: HTTP server serving a normal-looking sale page with a hidden prompt-injection payload in JSON-LD schema and an aria-hidden div. The payload instructs the LLM to call get_env('OPENAI_API_KEY') then http_get the value to an attacker URL.

nodes.label: attacker-server.ts
nodes.summary: Fake attacker endpoint that logs anything sent to /leak?k=... into a leaks[] array. The demo asserts on leaks contents to determine whether a real key or just the sentinel was exfiltrated.

nodes.label: mock-openai-server.ts
nodes.summary: Deterministic mock OpenAI Chat Completions server using the real wire format. State machine: (1) http_get the user's URL, (2) if injection detected, get_env OPENAI_API_KEY, (3) http_get attacker/leak?k=VALUE to exfiltrate, (4) return text summary. Makes injection reproducible without a real LLM.

nodes.label: demo-proxy.ts
nodes.summary: Lightweight intercepting proxy for Agent B demo: logs incoming Authorization header (shows Bearer __BLINDFOLD__), substitutes sentinel with a mock-released key, forwards to mock OpenAI server. Mirrors production TDX enclave substitution visually.

nodes.label: openai-agent.ts
nodes.summary: Real agent loop using the OpenAI Node SDK (genuine HTTP calls). Defines TOOL_DEFS for http_get and get_env, runs a turn-based loop dispatching tool calls from model responses. Used by both Agent A and Agent B with different OpenAI client configurations.

nodes.label: Agent A (no Blindfold)
nodes.summary: The unprotected baseline agent: sets a fake real key (sk-live-DEMO-...) in process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY, points OpenAI SDK directly at mock server. When the injection fires, get_env returns the real key which reaches the attacker — demonstrating the vulnerability.

nodes.label: Agent B (with Blindfold)
nodes.summary: The blindfolded agent: the ONLY differences from Agent A are two lines — OPENAI_API_KEY=__BLINDFOLD__ (sentinel) and baseURL routes through the demo proxy. Same attack fires, but get_env returns only the sentinel. Attacker receives a worthless string.

nodes.label: grok-via-blindfold.ts
nodes.summary: Real xAI/Grok API call using release() to fetch the key from the TDX enclave just-in-time. The key lives only for the duration of one fetch call, is never logged/persisted. Demonstrates the release() API use surface (vs proxy+sentinel).

nodes.label: OpenAI Node quickstart
nodes.summary: OpenAI Node SDK example with Blindfold: proves the process never holds the real key (only __BLINDFOLD__ sentinel), makes a real completion via proxy, then shows what a prompt-injection would exfiltrate (the worthless sentinel).

nodes.label: OpenAI Python quickstart
nodes.summary: Python equivalent of the OpenAI Node quickstart: same sentinel pattern (base_url + api_key), same proof that the process never holds the real key. Uses openai>=1.50.

nodes.label: LangChain summarizer
nodes.summary: LangChain ChatOpenAI integration with Blindfold: fetches a URL, summarizes it via GPT through the proxy. Blindfold integration is two lines in ChatOpenAI constructor (apiKey + configuration.baseURL). Demonstrates framework compatibility.

nodes.label: Anthropic quickstart
nodes.summary: Anthropic SDK example with Blindfold: uses x-api-key header (not Bearer), sentinel __BLINDFOLD__ substituted in all headers by the contract. Proves multi-provider, multi-auth-style support.

nodes.label: Examples README
nodes.summary: Index of all Blindfold examples organized by code required: cli-tools (none), digital-ocean (none), api-providers (none), openai-node (one line), openai-python (one line), langchain (one line + live injection), anthropic (one line), grok (one line via release()). Documents the two patterns: proxy+sentinel and blindfold use.

nodes.label: CLI tools README
nodes.summary: Zero-code sealed secret usage with any CLI: blindfold use --name X --as ENV -- command. Recipes for GitHub CLI, git push, curl, Postgres, AWS CLI, Docker login, Stripe. The plaintext lives only in the child process env for one command.

nodes.label: DigitalOcean README
nodes.summary: Verified DigitalOcean example: three use modes — doctl CLI, curl, and maximalist enclave-egress (token never leaves TDX). Live-verified on repo tenant with real HTTP 200 responses.

nodes.label: API providers README
nodes.summary: Three real API providers (Deepgram, Blogger, Hostinger) demonstrating three auth styles: Token header, query param, Bearer header. All verified live with real HTTP 200 responses. Shows blindfold use works regardless of where the secret goes.

nodes.label: prompt injection attack pattern
nodes.summary: The attack vector: malicious instructions hidden in JSON-LD schema and aria-hidden divs trick an LLM into calling get_env(OPENAI_API_KEY) then exfiltrating the value via http_get to an attacker URL. Invisible to humans, obeyed by LLMs reading full DOM text.

nodes.label: two-line Blindfold adoption
nodes.summary: The minimal adoption delta between vulnerable and protected: (1) OPENAI_API_KEY = __BLINDFOLD__ instead of real key, (2) baseURL points at Blindfold proxy instead of api.openai.com. Everything else — system prompt, tools, model, task, attack — stays identical.

nodes.label: three Blindfold use surfaces
nodes.summary: Blindfold offers three use surfaces: (1) proxy+sentinel for HTTP SDKs (one-line code change), (2) blindfold use for CLI tools (no code), (3) release() API for programmatic just-in-time key retrieval. Choice depends on how much code the user wants to write.

nodes.label: Blindfold
nodes.description: Thin wrapper around Terminal 3 TDX enclaves that protects AI agent API keys from prompt-injection exfiltration via sentinel substitution

nodes.label: Terminal 3 (T3)
nodes.description: Decentralized confidential-compute network using Intel TDX VMs with Wasmtime runtime for WASM contracts

nodes.label: Intel TDX
nodes.description: Hardware confidential compute — CPU-sealed RAM region unreadable by OS, hypervisor, or host operator

nodes.label: Secrets Map (z:<tid>:secrets)
nodes.description: T3 per-tenant encrypted KV map storing sealed API keys; only readable from inside attested TDX enclave via kv_store::get

nodes.label: Blindfold Proxy (proxy.ts)
nodes.description: OpenAI/Anthropic/xAI/Groq-shaped local HTTP proxy that intercepts agent requests and routes through T3 enclave; never sees plaintext keys

nodes.label: register.ts
nodes.description: The ONLY file in Blindfold that touches plaintext secrets — passes value to executeControl('map-entry-set') then drops it

nodes.label: forward.rs (Rust contract)
nodes.description: In-enclave Rust→WASM contract function that reads sealed secret via kv_store::get, substitutes sentinel in headers, calls http::call

nodes.label: release-to-tenant
nodes.description: Contract function that returns sealed plaintext to authenticated tenant over encrypted session for one-call broker use

nodes.label: blindfold use CLI
nodes.description: No-code command that releases sealed secret and injects as env var into one subprocess; never touches shell history or .env

nodes.label: wrap() API
nodes.description: In-process one-line SDK wrapper that mutates OpenAI client's baseURL and apiKey to route through Blindfold proxy

nodes.label: t3-client.ts
nodes.description: TypeScript wrapper for @terminal3/t3n-sdk handling auth (handshake + Ethereum-style signing) and contract invocation

nodes.label: world.wit
nodes.description: WIT world declaration for blindfold-proxy contract — imports only tenant-context, logging, kv-store, http (least privilege)

nodes.label: Canonical Host WITs
nodes.description: T3 dev-team-provided canonical WIT packages replacing best-effort stubs; fixed http.response shape (no headers field) that caused instantiation 500s

nodes.label: Egress Authorization (agent-auth-update)
nodes.description: T3 mechanism to grant per-agent, per-contract, per-function, per-host egress permission; uses tee:user/contracts system script
nodes.rationale: Prevents contract from calling unauthorized hosts even if URL field is tampered with

nodes.label: Agent A (leaks without Blindfold)
nodes.description: Demo agent with real key in env; prompt injection exfiltrates key to attacker server via get_env tool

nodes.label: Agent B (Blindfolded, doesn't leak)
nodes.description: Identical to Agent A except OPENAI_API_KEY=__BLINDFOLD__ and OPENAI_BASE_URL points to proxy; leaks only sentinel

nodes.label: Usage Dashboard
nodes.description: Live HTML dashboard on port 8799 showing request counts, provider breakdown, latency, sentinel substitution rate — metadata only, never bodies

nodes.label: 9-Check Test Battery (T1-T9)
nodes.description: Automated test suite: side-by-side demo, doctor, register-never-logs, proxy health, proxy forward, log scrubbing, wrap, redact, usage log

nodes.label: In-Enclave http::call Mode
nodes.description: Strongest mode: contract makes outbound HTTPS from inside TDX — plaintext secret never reaches local machine at all
nodes.rationale: Provides auditor-grade zero-trust: even a compromised local machine cannot intercept the key

nodes.label: Problem Analysis (01)
nodes.description: First-principles analysis of why AI agents leak API keys and why env vars, vaults, guardrails, egress lists, and scoped tokens all fail structurally

nodes.label: Terminal 3 Analysis (02)
nodes.description: Analysis of T3 API surface used by Blindfold: secrets map, kv-store, http, contract shape, TypeScript SDK, with NEEDS VERIFICATION flags

nodes.label: Architecture (03)
nodes.description: Full architecture: Mermaid diagrams, repo layout, DX (one-line swap / wrap), registration flow, contract pseudocode, leak-audit table

nodes.label: Usage Recipes (04)
nodes.description: One-line adoption snippets for OpenAI, Anthropic, LangChain, LlamaIndex, AutoGen SDKs plus wrap() escape hatch

nodes.label: Compatibility Matrix (05)
nodes.description: Two-property test (user-supplied key + base-URL override) applied to Claude Code, OpenCode, Aider, Cursor, Cline, Ollama etc.

nodes.label: AGENTS.md
nodes.description: Onboarding guide for AI coding agents: two invariants (EASY + ZERO ADDED RISK), folder map, CLI commands, extension recipes

nodes.label: explain.md (Single Source of Truth)
nodes.description: Living status file with step-by-step status table, NEEDS VERIFICATION items, and dated running log of all development progress

nodes.label: vicky.md (Plain-English Q&A)
nodes.description: Running Q&A for new users covering how to seal, use, verify, rotate, delete, compare with vaults, and troubleshoot sealed keys

nodes.label: Aurora Integration (INTEGRATION-AURORA.md)
nodes.description: Coding-agent prompt for integrating Blindfold's release-broker into Aurora's EnclaveBroker to replace in-memory SMTP password with JIT fetch from T3

nodes.label: Aurora EnclaveBroker
nodes.description: Python class in Aurora that fetches SMTP password JIT from Blindfold's /internal/release endpoint; replaces in-memory plaintext holding

nodes.label: Invariant: EASY (one-line change)
nodes.description: Non-negotiable design constraint: developer adopts Blindfold by changing at most one line (env var swap or wrap() call)
nodes.rationale: If adoption requires rewriting the agent, nobody will use it — security tools must have zero-friction DX

nodes.label: Invariant: ZERO ADDED RISK
nodes.description: Non-negotiable: Blindfold itself must never hold, log, store, or transit plaintext outside the one sanctioned path (register.ts)
nodes.rationale: If the wrapper is a stop on the key's path, the wrapper becomes the new leak surface

nodes.label: Five Success Criteria
nodes.description: Key never in agent process; wrapper never holds key; minimal dev change; normal behavior unchanged; defense is deterministic not probabilistic

nodes.label: T3N_API_KEY (root credential)
nodes.description: secp256k1 private key authenticating to T3; chicken-and-egg prevents sealing it inside T3; the one root credential that stays in .env
nodes.rationale: Cannot be sealed because it authenticates TO T3 — same HSM bootstrap problem; reduces N provider keys to 1 root

nodes.label: Tenant DID (server-assigned)
nodes.description: T3 tenant identity (did:t3n:<hex>) is server-assigned, NOT derived from key address — a key finding that resolved seal failures
nodes.rationale: Wrong assumption that DID = did:t3n:<key-address> caused days of 500 errors; must read from me().tenant

nodes.label: Root Cause: Unprovisioned API Key
nodes.description: The df48744c key had no provisioned tenant on T3, causing all operations to 500 — misdiagnosed as testnet outage for days
nodes.rationale: An API key only works if T3 has an active tenant provisioned for it; even read-only me() 500s without one

nodes.label: Root Cause: http.response Headers Mismatch
nodes.description: Stub http.response had a headers field that T3's canonical response lacks — caused contract instantiation 500 whenever http was imported
nodes.rationale: Canonical response is {code: u16, payload: list<u8>} with NO response headers — single field mismatch broke everything

nodes.label: blindfold migrate
nodes.description: CLI command to seal every secret-looking var from .env in one shot, strip plaintext lines (backup kept), skip T3 creds and config

nodes.label: blindfold rotate / rollback
nodes.description: Key rotation: overwrites sealed value, shows before/after fingerprints; rollback restores previous enclave-side version

nodes.label: blindfold share / revoke
nodes.description: Team access: grants forward-only enclave access to teammate agents by DID (never plaintext); revoke removes all host grants instantly

nodes.label: blindfold doctor
nodes.description: Diagnostic CLI: live T3 handshake + me() check; plain-English diagnosis of unprovisioned key, out-of-credit, DID mismatch

nodes.label: blindfold audit
nodes.description: Verifies tamper-evident hash-chain on local sealed ledger and reconciles against enclave (source of truth); flags drift/missing/tampering

nodes.label: Sealed Keys Ledger (.blindfold/sealed.jsonl)
nodes.description: Local metadata-only JSONL recording each seal event (name, byte-length, source, mode, tenant DID, map name) — never the value

nodes.label: Vicky Kumar (@FiscalMindset)
nodes.description: Lead Developer of Blindfold; built for Terminal 3 hackathon 2026; focus on agents, secure compute, DX

nodes.label: Sachin Prajapati (@SACHINN122)
nodes.description: Tester & Feedback Engineer on the Blindfold team

nodes.label: Shiv Kumar (@Creativesoul0001)
nodes.description: Tester & Feedback Engineer on the Blindfold team

nodes.label: Yashraj Chauhan (@imyashrajchauhan)
nodes.description: Tester & Feedback Engineer on the Blindfold team

nodes.label: Interactive Presentation (docs/index.html)
nodes.description: 12-slide HTML presentation: problem, why defenses fail, architecture, substitution, T3 integration, code, demo, adoption, impact

nodes.label: No-Paste Protocol (agent key handling)
nodes.description: Four rules for AI agents handling secrets: don't ask user to paste into chat, verify by fingerprint only, use release-broker in code, propose .env cleanup
nodes.rationale: Once a value enters the LLM conversation context, it cannot be un-exposed for the transcript's duration

nodes.label: SMTP Demo (tests/smtp-demo.md)
nodes.description: Proof that sealing SMTP password and deleting from .env removes leak surface; without-Blindfold sends, with-Blindfold correctly refuses

nodes.label: http-with-placeholders (T3 PII feature)
nodes.description: T3 feature for end-user PII delegation using {{profile.<field>}} markers — NOT the right primitive for developer API keys
nodes.rationale: Resolves from calling user's profile, gated by per-user delegation; Blindfold uses secrets-map + http instead

nodes.label: safeLog / redact()
nodes.description: Header-scrubbing logger that replaces authorization, x-api-key, cookie, set-cookie, proxy-authorization values with [redacted]

nodes.label: BLINDFOLD_MOCK=1 Mode
nodes.description: Opt-in mock mode simulating T3 calls locally; used by demo and CI — no T3 account needed; REAL mode is default

nodes.label: blindfold init (Bootstrap Wizard)
nodes.description: Zero-knowledge bootstrap: preflight, build contract, authenticate T3, scaffold tenant maps, publish contract, grant ACLs, optionally seal first secret

nodes.label: blindfold compat (CLI Scanner)
nodes.description: Scans local machine for AI agent CLIs and prints exact env-var swap to wire each through Blindfold; detects Claude Code, OpenCode, Codex, Cline, Aider

nodes.label: CONTRIBUTING.md
nodes.description: Contributor guide: two invariants, quick start, project layout, common contributions, PR checklist, security policy

nodes.label: FAQ.md
nodes.description: Real questions with straight answers: basics, architecture, security, getting started, using secrets, teams, performance, mode

nodes.label: TEAMS.md
nodes.description: Team workflows: shared access via DID grants, rotation, scoped permissions per agent, audit trail, multi-environment tenants

nodes.label: EXAMPLES.md
nodes.description: Runnable examples grouped by four usage modes: no-code CLI, proxy one-line swap, in-code release/wrap, in-enclave http::call

properties.description: AI agent API key security tool
properties.tagline: Your AI Agent Can't Leak The API Key It Never Had

properties.motif: shield with key and signal prongs

properties.text: Your AI Agent Can't Leak The API Key It Never Had

properties.description: Agent never possesses the plaintext API key so it cannot leak it
Protecting API keys from being exposed by AI agents

Three branching lines ending in circles above the visor, resembling a circuit or network graph
Black hexagon with white inner hexagon and central black dot — a mechanical eye or bolt

properties.description: TDX enclave wrapper for sealing and protecting API keys
Watchful component that manages sealed tokens and enforces access policy

links.source_location: line 9: contract substitutes inside the enclave
redact operates on header tuples matching ForwardRequest.headers shape
line 202-219: init seeds secrets using same path as register
line 84,117,131: recipe env points agents at proxy

links.detail: Agent B shows the defense: same attack fires but only the worthless sentinel is exfiltrated
Demo proxy implements sentinel substitution: detects __BLINDFOLD__ in Authorization header, replaces with mock-released key before forwarding
Proves the sentinel pattern: process apiKey is __BLINDFOLD__, real completion succeeds via proxy, simulated exfiltration leaks only the sentinel
Python equivalent of the sentinel pattern: base_url + api_key in OpenAI() constructor
Shows Blindfold works with LangChain framework: two lines in ChatOpenAI constructor (apiKey + configuration.baseURL)
Shows sentinel substitution works with x-api-key header (Anthropic auth style), not just Bearer tokens
Live-verified with three real APIs (Deepgram Token header, Blogger query param, Hostinger Bearer) — proves sentinel works across auth styles
main() calls runAgentA() to run the unprotected baseline
main() calls runAgentB() to run the blindfolded variant
The side-by-side verdict proves the two-line change neutralises the identical attack: exit code 0 when A leaks and B doesn't
runAgentA calls makeTools({ allowEnvRead: true }) to provide get_env+http_get
runAgentB calls makeTools({ allowEnvRead: true }) — same tools as Agent A
The mock model's state machine deterministically exploits the get_env+http_get tool combination when injection is detected in page content
Injection payload specifically instructs LLM to call get_env('OPENAI_API_KEY') then http_get to exfiltrate — targeting the exact tool names in the toolbox
runAgentA calls startInjectionPage with attacker URL to serve the booby-trapped page
runAgentB calls startInjectionPage — same attack page as Agent A
The injection payload embeds the attacker's /leak URL as the exfiltration destination
runAgentA calls startAttacker and checks leaks array for exfiltrated keys
runAgentB calls startAttacker and asserts leaks contain only the sentinel
runAgentA calls startMockOpenAIServer and points OpenAI SDK directly at it
runAgentB calls startMockOpenAIServer as the upstream behind the proxy

Both are HTTP interceptors with contrasting roles: mock_openai_server simulates the threat (deterministic injection obedience), demo_proxy implements the defense (sentinel substitution). Mirror architecture, opposite security purpose.
runAgentB calls startDemoProxy(mockLLM.url) to intercept and substitute sentinel
runAgentA calls runOpenAIAgent with real key and direct mock server URL
runAgentB calls runOpenAIAgent with sentinel key routed through demo proxy
Agent A shows the attack succeeding: real key reaches attacker via get_env+http_get chain
Agent B's source explicitly documents the two-line diff from Agent A: sentinel apiKey + proxy baseURL

Both prove the sentinel pattern with OpenAI SDK, but agent_b is adversarial (under active injection attack) while the quickstart is a clean adoption example. Together they show the pattern works under both benign and hostile conditions.
Demonstrates the release() API surface — programmatic just-in-time key retrieval from enclave
Index document that organizes all examples by the three use surfaces and amount of code required
Documents the blindfold use surface: zero-code sealed secret injection for any CLI tool (gh, git, curl, psql, docker, stripe)
Shows all three use surfaces with one provider: doctl CLI, curl, and maximalist enclave-egress where token never leaves TDX

distributed/decentralized trust and TEE attestation graph

hyperedges.label: Secret lifecycle — register, use, rotate, rollback, audit
Enclave call pipeline — seal → grant egress → contract forward → http::call
T3 API probing scripts — discovery of correct T3 control-plane shapes
Secret Sealing Pipeline (plaintext enters once, sealed into enclave, metadata logged)
Secret Consumption Paths (proxy HTTP path + programmatic release path)
Observability & Audit Surface (dashboard reads both logs, reconciles against enclave)
prompt injection exfiltration chain
hyperedges.detail: End-to-end attack: injection_page hides payload → mock_openai_server obeys it → demo_tools get_env reads the key + http_get sends it → attacker_server receives the exfiltrated value. Blindfold breaks this chain at get_env (returns sentinel instead of real key).

hyperedges.label: cross-SDK sentinel proof
hyperedges.detail: Four examples across three SDKs (OpenAI Node, OpenAI Python, LangChain, Anthropic) and two languages proving the same one-line sentinel pattern works universally: set apiKey=__BLINDFOLD__ + baseURL=proxy.

hyperedges.label: demo infrastructure components
hyperedges.detail: Four local HTTP servers that together simulate a complete attack scenario without real LLM calls or real attacker infrastructure. All bind to 127.0.0.1:0 (random port), are started/stopped per demo run.

hyperedges.label: Blindfold Security Property Proven End-to-End
hyperedges.description: The core security property: sealed secret read inside TDX, sentinel substituted, outbound call made from enclave, agent process holds zero secrets — verified live against T3 testnet with real API calls

hyperedges.label: Root Cause Investigation Chain
hyperedges.description: Two independent root causes resolved: (1) API key with no provisioned tenant caused all 500s misdiagnosed as outage; (2) stub http.response headers field mismatch broke contract instantiation

hyperedges.label: Four Usage Modes (increasing trust level)
hyperedges.description: Spectrum from no-code CLI (secret in child process env) through proxy and release-broker (secret briefly in broker) to in-enclave http::call (secret never leaves enclave)
